description: To present a proposition publicly, for others to consider, question, or challenge. A claim is the public act; 'nohero' (believe) is what happens privately and needs no audience at all. A person can claim something provisionally, or even repeat a claim they reject, purely to hold it up for examination.
sound_symbolism: Hushed 'she' shapes the first breath before it leaves the mouth. The tongue flicks through stressed 're' and gives the sound a direction beyond the speaker. At the end, 'wa' rounds at the lips and opens into broad 'a': controlled breath has crossed a living connection and arrived in shared air.
grammatical_notes: A transitive verb. A nominal object names what is claimed; a full proposition uses the declarative complement frame: 'mia mena ha ruela welao nai meno sherewa.' (I claim that this path is good.) The event-noun rule supplies a claim as a noun. Distinct from 'nohero' (believe), an internal stance, and 'shane' (tell), the act of giving an account.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Treating a claim as an act rather than an identity makes room for revision: a speaker can release or change a claim without treating the self as fixed by it.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A dedicated verb for claiming lets speakers locate disagreement in a proposition instead of turning it into a judgment of the person who voiced it.

semantic_domains.cognition: a proposition deliberately put forward for assessment
semantic_domains.communication: the public act of offering a proposition to others
